title: Dual-host git-hook shims ping-pong between plugin roots; uninstalling one plugin silently fail-opens git-level enforcement installed by it for the other host
evidence: The pre-commit/pre-push shim embeds the ABSOLUTE enforcer path of whichever plugin last ran SessionStart (`E="{enforcer}"` from `_enforcer_path()` = this vendored copy's dir), and the shim is deliberately fail-open when that file is missing: `[ -f "$E" ] || exit 0`. With ca and ca-codex both installed against one repo, every alternation of host rewrites both shims (plus the .git/codearbiter-hooksdir-cache) to point into the OTHER plugin's install dir. If the user then uninstalls or prunes whichever plugin most recently wrote the shim (e.g. removes the BETA ca-codex), the shim's enforcer path dangles and every commit/push passes the git-level backstop with exit 0 — even though the surviving plugin is installed, active, and would have enforced — until that surviving host's next SessionStart happens to rewrite the shim.
impact: A window (potentially long, e.g. Codex-was-last-used then only git CLI is used) where the #161 git-level enforcement backstop is silently unwired for BOTH hosts after removing ONE plugin. The single-plugin fail-open was an accepted 'our OWN staleness' trade; dual-host widens it to a cross-plugin dependency the user has no reason to expect.
recommendation: Make the shim resilient to one plugin's removal: probe both known plugin roots, or point at a host-neutral copy, or fall back to warning loudly (non-zero-comment) instead of bare exit 0 when the pinned enforcer is gone while a sibling plugin's enforcer exists.
acceptance_criteria: With both plugins installed and shims last written by ca-codex, deleting the ca-codex install dir leaves commits still gated (or loudly warned), given ca remains installed
